Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study, but then I'd just drift through the day doing other things. When I did sit down, I learn better by doing stuff with my hands, like working through problems physically, but the course wasn't really set up for that. I'd maybe spend a few hours here and there, but it was scattered and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly remember a deadline and panic-study. There wasn't any regularity to it. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and I just couldn't find the energy to stick to a schedule. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I saw a notification. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important. It wasn't like I was excited to be there or anything. I'd also sometimes log in when I was feeling guilty about not doing enough, but then I'd just browse around without really doing much.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. I'd have brief periods where I was logging in more, especially when I was chatting with classmates in the discussions, but it never stuck as a habit. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped my mood, but it didn't translate into me suddenly becoming organized about the LMS.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually okay because I could interact with other people and that kept me somewhat engaged. I posted a decent amount and talked to classmates there. The videos were useful when I actually watched them because I could pause and rewind. But honestly, nothing felt super useful because I wasn't motivated enough to really dig into any of it properly.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the readings and supplementary materials. They just felt like walls of text, and I'm not great with that kind of learning. I need to be doing something, not just reading. I'd open them sometimes and skim, but I rarely engaged deeply. The stuff that required me to just sit and absorb information passively got pushed aside constantly.
Q7: If I could see an immediate point to it, like it directly connected to an assignment grade, I'd do it. Or if it involved actually doing something interactive rather than just reading or watching. The discussions felt worth my time because I was talking to real people. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, I'd skip.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. I already struggled to do the required things, so optional activities were completely off my radar. Required stuff at least had the pressure of grades behind it, which was sometimes the only thing that could push me to act.
Q9: Sometimes I'd download stuff and look at it on my phone later, away from the computer. I'd also occasionally write out notes by hand because that helps me remember better than typing. I don't have a lot of resources, so I'd try to make do with what I could access offline. But honestly, even my offline studying was pretty minimal.
Q10: There were times I'd be thinking about course concepts while doing other things, like working or just walking around. I'd mentally go over problems or try to understand something I'd read earlier. None of that shows up anywhere. Also, sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, talking through stuff, and that wouldn't register on any system.
Q11: Yeah, I did that with some of the videos. I'd watch them once online, maybe take some notes, and then not go back to the LMS version. I'd just work from my notes. With limited internet sometimes, it was easier to grab things once and then work offline. The system wouldn't know I was still using that material.
Q12: There were definitely low points. I was feeling pretty sad a lot of the time, and that made everything harder. Getting out of bed to do coursework felt impossible some days. When my anxiety spiked about falling behind, I'd paradoxically avoid the course even more. The mindfulness intervention helped my mood eventually, but by then I'd already missed so much.
Q13: Absolutely. I was tired a lot, and I had other responsibilities that ate into my time. When I was confused about something in the course, I'd just shut down instead of seeking help. The workload felt overwhelming even though I wasn't doing most of it. My low resources meant I was sometimes dealing with stuff that other students probably didn't have to worry about.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd be logged in, clicking through things, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video but not absorb anything because I was too distracted or tired. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussions without really reading them. It looked like engagement but it was just going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd still put them off. Part of it was anxiety—the thought of starting something and failing felt worse than just not starting. Part of it was low motivation; I just couldn't make myself care enough in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and go.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the course for days, then scramble when something was due. My LMS activity probably spiked right before deadlines and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only external force strong enough to overcome my lack of internal motivation.
Q17: When I actually submitted something and got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. But often I'd already moved on mentally or the feedback came too late to matter for my learning. If the feedback was negative, it sometimes made me feel worse and less likely to engage. It's complicated—I wanted feedback but also dreaded it.
Q18: The structure didn't really work for me. It seemed designed for people who could self-motivate and stay on top of things independently. I needed more hands-on stuff, more immediate accountability. The way it was set up, I could easily fall through the cracks, and I did. Weekly modules just blurred together when I wasn't attending regularly.
Q19: The lack of interactive, hands-on activities made it harder for me. I learn by doing, and there wasn't much of that. The discussions were one of the few things that engaged me because they involved actual interaction. But overall, the design assumed a type of learner I'm not. More structured check-ins might have helped me stay on track.
Q20: I can relate to that actually. Sometimes you're logging in, clicking around, watching stuff, participating in discussions, but you're not actually producing work. You might be overwhelmed, confused about what's expected, or just procrastinating in a way that looks productive. High activity doesn't mean you're actually accomplishing the things that get graded.
Q21: I guess some people are just efficient—they know exactly what they need to do, do it quickly, and don't spend extra time on the platform. That wasn't me though. My low activity matched my low performance. But I can see how someone who studies effectively offline or already knows the material wouldn't need to be on the LMS much.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on. It can track time spent on videos and forum posts. But it completely misses whether you actually understood anything, whether you were mentally present, or whether you were learning in ways that don't involve the platform. It's like measuring presence without measuring learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. For me, real engagement would mean being interested, asking questions, doing the work because I want to learn, not just because I have to. I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was just surviving.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how sad I was feeling most of the time, or how my anxiety made me avoid things. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness sessions that actually helped my mood improve toward the end. It wouldn't show that I was dealing with limited resources and other life pressures. The numbers just see a failing student, not the whole picture of why.
